paper_title: Towards a Strategy for Economic Growth in Uruguay
research_answer: The devaluations of Brazil (January 1999) and Argentina (December 2001) hit Uruguay with a double blow. First, they shifted relative prices sharply against Uruguayan goods. Second — and equally damaging — both events were accompanied by major collapses in domestic demand in those countries. Since Uruguay had oriented much of its tradable production toward Mercosur integration during the appreciated-RER years of the 1990s, it was acutely exposed to this regional demand shock. The result was a major reduction in regional demand for Uruguayan goods, compounding the price competitiveness loss. This episode illustrates the vulnerability of tying export strategy to regional markets with unstable macroeconomic conditions.
supporting_quotes: This pattern was first broken in January 1999 when Brazil devalued and later in December 2001 when Argentina did the same. Both events involved in addition to a shift of relative prices, an important decline in aggregate demand in the two countries. As a consequence, Uruguay was hit by a major reduction in regional demand for its goods.

data_points.metric: impact on regional demand for Uruguayan goods
